_meta.name: Self Discovery Frameworks
_meta.description: Frameworks for understanding yourself and what matters to you
_meta.use_when: Content focuses on self understanding, values clarification, or life assessment

source.creator: Various, commonly associated with William R. Miller (Motivational Interviewing tradition)
source.publication: Various MI and acceptance based therapy resources
source.field: Motivational interviewing, acceptance and commitment therapy

descriptions.professional: A structured exercise for values clarification in which individuals sort a set of value cards into categories of importance, then prioritise their top values. The exercise externalises the abstract process of values identification, making it more concrete and less cognitively demanding. Values clarification is associated with improved wellbeing, clearer decision making and enhanced motivation for change.
descriptions.peer_led: Values are what matters most to you, what you want your life to be about. A values card sort is a simple way to figure out what your values are. You sort cards with different values into piles based on how important each one is to you. Then you pick your top ones. Knowing your values helps you make decisions, understand why some things bother you, and live a life that feels meaningful.

background.origin: Values work has roots in humanistic psychology and has been developed extensively within motivational interviewing and acceptance and commitment therapy.
background.evidence_base: Values clarification is associated with positive outcomes in therapy and behaviour change. The process of articulating values can strengthen commitment to aligned actions.
background.why_it_matters: Many people struggle to articulate their values. Having clear values aids decision making, increases motivation and provides a sense of meaning.
background.limitations: Values can conflict with each other. Cultural differences affect value expression. Values may change over time.

process.detail: Provide a comprehensive list of values (30 to 80 typically).
Sort into three piles: Very Important, Somewhat Important, Not Important.
From the Very Important pile, narrow to your top 10, then top 5.
Put your top 5 values in order.
For each top value, reflect on what it means to you and how you live it.

source.creator: Unknown, widely attributed to Paul J. Meyer and coaching tradition
source.publication: Various coaching and personal development resources

descriptions.professional: A visual self assessment tool that measures subjective satisfaction across multiple life domains. The wheel metaphor illustrates that imbalance in any area creates a bumpy ride through life. The assessment identifies areas of relative strength and areas requiring attention, facilitating goal setting and prioritisation.
descriptions.peer_led: The Wheel of Life is a way to take a snapshot of how satisfied you are with different areas of your life right now. You rate areas like work, relationships, health and fun on a scale. Then you plot them on a wheel and see the shape. A bumpy wheel shows where life feels out of balance. It helps you see clearly where you might want to focus your energy.

background.origin: Widely used in coaching and personal development. The exact origin is unclear but it became popular through life coaching programmes.
background.evidence_base: Face valid and widely used, though limited formal research. Aligns with concepts of life balance and wellbeing.
background.why_it_matters: Creates a visual overview of life satisfaction. Helps identify priorities for change. Tracks progress over time. Simple and accessible.
background.limitations: Categories may not fit everyone. Subjective ratings can fluctuate. Does not capture complexity of how areas interact.

typical_categories.name: Career or Work
typical_categories.description: Satisfaction with your work, career progress, sense of purpose in work

typical_categories.name: Finances
typical_categories.description: Satisfaction with your financial situation, security, relationship with money

typical_categories.name: Health and Fitness
typical_categories.description: Satisfaction with physical health, fitness, energy levels, self care

typical_categories.name: Family and Friends
typical_categories.description: Satisfaction with family relationships and friendships, sense of belonging

typical_categories.name: Romance or Partner
typical_categories.description: Satisfaction with romantic relationship or dating life

typical_categories.name: Personal Growth
typical_categories.description: Satisfaction with learning, development, becoming who you want to be

typical_categories.name: Fun and Recreation
typical_categories.description: Satisfaction with leisure, hobbies, play, enjoyment

typical_categories.name: Physical Environment
typical_categories.description: Satisfaction with home, surroundings, possessions, living space

process.detail: Score each life area from 1 to 10 based on current satisfaction.
Mark your score on each spoke of the wheel and connect the dots.
Notice which areas are high and which are low.
Choose one or two areas to focus on.
For your priority areas, set specific goals.

suggested_combinations.use_for: Comprehensive life assessment and direction setting
suggested_combinations.how: Use values card sort to clarify what matters. Use wheel of life to assess current satisfaction. Compare to identify gaps between values and life.
